content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Intere
(916) 322-5562 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

April 28, 1982

Owen Lee Kwong
Deputy Attorney General
3580 Wilshire Blvd.
Los Angeles, CA 90010

Re: Our No. A-82-076

Dear Mr. Kwong:

Thank you for your request of March 30, 1982, regarding
the application of the financial disclosure provisions of
the Political Reform Act. You have posed the following
question:

Whether the financial disclosure requirements of
the Government Conflict of Interests Act (Gov. Code,
Section 87100, et seq.,) as implemented in the California
Department of Justice "1980-81 Manual for Statements of
Economic Interests for Designated Employees Form 730
(JUS 501 (1/81))," as applied to Department of Justice
designated attorneys, infringes upon the Supreme Court's
inherent and exclusive power to regulate conduct of
attorneys and violates the State Constitution?

It is our conclusion that, as a deputy attorney general,
you are required to comply with the financial disclosure and
disqualification provisions of the Political Reform Act and
the Department of Justice's Conflict of Interest Code. We
further conclude that these provisions, as applied to Department
of Justice attorneys, do not infringe upon the Supreme Court's
inherent power to regulate the conduct of attorneys and do
not violate the State Constitution.

The Political Reform Act clearly includes government
attorneys within its conflict of interest provisions. The
disclosure provisions of Article 2 (Government Code Sections
87200-87210-) specifically apply to the Attorney General
(who is an elected state officer, Sections 82021, 82024) and

1/ All statutory references made are to the Government
Code unless otherwise noted.

Owen Lee Kwong
April 28, 1982
Page Two


to district attorneys. Section 87200.2/ Under Section
87300, the Department of Justice is an "agency" which must
adopt a Conflict of Interest Code. See Section 82049. In
that Code, deputy attorneys general may be required to be
designated. See Sections 87302, 82019. The Department of
Justice has, in fact, adopted a Conflict of Interest Code
pursuant to Section 87300, under which you are designated as
a deputy attorney general, and are required to file an annual
financial disclosure statement. This Code has the force of
law. Section 87300. Unless the disclosure provision of
this Code, as applied to you, are found to violate the State
or Federal Constitutions,3/ you must file a public statement
of economic interests as required by the Code and by the
Political Reform Act. Failure to comply may make you liable
for administrative, civil or criminal penalties. Sections
83116, 91000, 91005.

Although the California courts have not specifically
addressed the issue you raise in your letter, the "general
operation" of the Political Reform Act's financial disclosure
provisions have been upheld by the California Supreme Court
against constitutional challenge. Hays v. Wood, 25 Cal.3d
772, 778, 160 Cal.Rptr. 102 (1979). In Hays v. Wood the
Court held that public officials who are attorneys may not
constitutionally be required to make greater disclosure of
financial interests than other officials. At the same time,
the Court clearly held that officials who are attorneys can
be required to make the same disclosure as other public
officials. 25 Cal.3d at 780-795. Thus, Hays v. Wood demon-
strates that the California Supreme Court did not feel that
its power to regulate and discipline attorneys required
exemption of public officials who are attorneys from
the financial disclosure provisions of the Political Reform
Act. Although Hays v. Wood does not deal directly with
government attorneys (only with private attorneys who are

2/ Judges of Courts of Record are also specifically
covered by Section 87200.

3/ Article 3, Section 3.5 of the California Constitution
states that an administrative agency cannot declare a statute
unconstitutional or refuse to enforce a statute on constitu-
tional grounds. There might be a problem with this section
if we were to agree that the constitution prohibits your
inclusion as a designated employee in your agency's Code.
However, since we conclude that the relevant portions of the
Act are constitutional, Section 3.5 poses no problems.

Owen Lee Kwong
April 28, 1982
Page Three


also public officials), the Supreme Court has upheld finan-
cial disclosure requirements for specified State officials,
including the California Attorney General, under a previous
conflict of interest law. County of Nevada v. MacMillan, 11
Cal.3d 662, 667-674. If the Court did not deem financial
disclosure requirements for the Attorney General to be an
infringement upon its inherent power to regulate and discipline
attorneys, it is difficult to see why the Court would reach
a different conclusion with respect to the Attorney General's
deputies.

In your letter, you refer to two Pennsylvania cases.
Ballou v. State Ethics Comm. (1981) 424 A.2d 983, and Kremer
v. State Ethics Comm. (1981) 424 A.2d 963. These are both
decisions of Pennsylvania's intermediate appellate court,
the Commonwealth Court. Both were appealed to Pennsylvania's
Supreme Court. Ballou has been decided by that court, 436
A.2d 186 (1981). I could not find a reported decision in
Kremer.

The Commonwealth Court in Ballou held that the statutory
requirements for financial disclosure by state attorneys
violated the Pennsylvania Supreme Court's inherent and exclusive
constitutional power to regulate and discipline attorneys.
On appeal, however, the Supreme Court did not adopt the
Commonwealth Court's rationale. Instead, it ruled that
contract government attorneys were not "public employees" or
"public officials" within the meaning of the Pennsylvania
Statute. It did not reach the Constitutional issue. The
Commonwealth Court, in the Kremer case, held that judges
could not be required to comply with the Act's disclosure
provisions because that would violate the Supreme Court's
inherent and exclusive constitutional power to regulate
judges.

Thus the Pennsylvania cases cannot be said to stand for
the proposition that it is unconstitutional under Pennsylvania
law for a statute to require financial disclosure by government
attorneys. However, even if the Ballou rationale had been
adopted by the Pennsylvania Supreme Court, it would not have
been useful in providing guidance on this issue to the California
courts because, unlike the Pennsylvania Court, the California
Supreme Court does not have exclusive power to regulate and
discipline attorneys.

The California Constitution does not explicitly give
the California Supreme Court the power to regulate and discipline

_______________
4/ As discussed above, California's government attorneys
are covered by the Political Reform Act.

Owen Lee Kwong
April 28, 1982
Page Four


attorneys. Nevertheless, the California Supreme Court has
repeatedly held that the Constitutional grant of judicial
power (Constitution, Article VI, Section 1) gives it "inherent
power over the admission, disbarment and suspension of attorneys."
Stratoore v. State Bar (1975) 14 Cal.3d 887, 889. However,
the Court has held equally often that this inherent power is
not exclusive.

However, notwithstanding the inherent power of the
courts to admit applicants for licenses to practice law
it is generally conceded that the legislature may prescribe
reasonable rules and regulations for admission to the
bar which will be followed by the courts.... The courts,
in the exercise of their inherent power may demand more
than the legislature has required.

In re Lavine (1935) 2 Cal.2d
324, 328.

It is clear not only that the legislature (or the people,
by the initiative process) can regulate the conduct of attorneys
by statute without infringing on the Supreme Court's inherent
powers, but also that it has often exercised this power
without challenge.2/ There are only two limitations on this
legislative power. First, the legislative requirements can
only be "minimum" requirements, and cannot limit the power
of the Court to impose more stringent requirements upon attorneys.
Stratoore v. State Bar, supra at 389-890. Second, legislative
requirements will be upheld only to the extent that they do
not conflict with the court's own rules. Merco Constr.
Engineers, Inc. v. Municipal Court (1978) 21 Cal.3d 724,
728-729.

The financial disclosure and disqualification requirements of the Political Reform Act do not attempt to limit
the Supreme Court's power to impose more stringent requirements of its own (see Section 81013), nor do they conflict
with any existing court rules. Thus, they do not infringe
upon the court's inherent constitutional powers. This is
apparently the view of the Supreme Court. If the Supreme
Court had felt that the Political Reform Act's disclosure
provisions intruded upon its powers, it would not have approved
those provisions, as applied to attorneys, in Hays v. Wood.

5/ The joint nature of the judicial and legislative
authority to regulate the conduct of attorneys is demonstrated by the fact that there are both court rules and
numerous statutes governing attorneys in California. See
Business and Professions Code, Chapter 4. Among these statutes
there is a requirement for financial disclosure by attorneys.
Bus. & Prof. Code Section 6069.

Owen Lee Kwong
April 28, 1982
Page Five


     In view of the conclusions set forth in this letter,
your Statement of Economic Interests, which you state you
filed under seal on or before April 1, 1982, must be made
available for public inspection and copying.  Section 81003.
In addition, in the future, you will have to file Statements
of Economic Interests as required by your Department's Conflict
of Interest Code.

     I hope this has answered your questions.  If you wish
to discuss this further, feel free to call me at (916)
322/5901.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [illegible]
                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

BAM:km

cc:  Robert Blasier
     James D. Mosman, Code Filing Officer
     Scott W. Thorpe, Deputy Attorney General

State of California

Memorandum

To : Commissioners Conrad, Houston,
McAndrews, Metzger and Ziffren

Date : April 13, 1982

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
Barbara A. Milman

Subject: Opinion Request -- Owen Lee Kwong

Owen Kwong, a deputy attorney general, has submitted an opinion
request (attached), in which he challenges the requirement that he
file a financial disclosure statement under the Political Reform
Act. The staff recommends that the opinion request be denied, and
that Mr. Kwong's request be answered by a staff advice letter.

Mr. Kwong contends that it is unconstitutional to require a
deputy attorney general (or any other government-employed attorney)
to make financial disclosure under the Act, because, he claims, the
State Constitution confers exclusive authority upon the California
Supreme Court to regulate the conduct of, or require disclosure by,
government attorneys. However, the Political Reform Act explicitly
provides that government attorneys (district attorneys as well as
the attorney general) are covered by its disclosure and disqualifi-
cation provisions. Government Code Sections 82003, 82004, 82019,
82024, 87200, 87302.

To exclude government attorneys from the coverage of the Act
would go against long-standing Commission policy and practice, as
embodied in numerous conflict of interest codes, Commission opinions
and advice letters. In addition, such an exclusion, if based on
constitutional grounds, would raise constitution problems of its
own under Article 3, Section 3.5 of the State Constitution.— Thus

1/ An administrative agency, including an administrative
agency created by the Constitution or an initiative statute, has no
power:

(a) To declare a statute unenforceable, or refuse to
enforce a statute, on the basis of it being unconstitutional
unless an appellate court has made a determination that such
statute is unconstitutional;

(b) To declare a statute unconstitutional;

(c) To declare a statute unenforceable, or to refuse to
enforce a statute on the basis that federal law or federal
regulations prohibit the enforcement of such statute unless an
appellate court has made a determination that the enforcement
of such statute is prohibited by federal law or federal regulations.

State Constitution Article 3, Section 3.5.

Commissioners Conrad, Houston,
McAndrews, Metzger and Ziffren
April 13, 1982
Page Two


the Commission's answer to Mr. Kwong's question must be that he is
required to make financial disclosure under the Act. Nevertheless,
the staff would like to respond to Mr. Kwong's opinion request with
a letter explaining in some detail why the challenged provisions of
the Political Reform Act are, in fact, constitutional. We hope, by
such a response, to satisfy Mr. Kwong, and to dissuade him from
pursuing this matter further.

If I do not hear from you by April 19, I will assume that you
agree with the staff's recommendation.

BAM:plh:32-4
Attachment

State of California
Department of Justice
George Deukmejian
(PRONOUNCED DUKE MAY-GIN)
Attorney General

3580 WILSHIRE BLVD.
LOS ANGELES 90010
(213) 736-2304

March 30, 1982
Request No. 82-002

Robert Stern, General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street, P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Date Received March 31, 1982

Response Due _________

Re: Request for Opinion
Government Code section 83114, 2 California
Administrative Code section 18320, et seq.

Dear Mr. Stern:

Question Posed: Whether the financial disclosure
requirements of the Government Conflict of
Interests Act (Gov. Code, section 87100 et
seq.) as implemented in the California
Department of Justice "1980-81 Manual for
Statements of Economic Interests for
Designated Employees form 730 (JUS 501
(1/81))," as applied to Department of
Justice designated attorneys, infringes upon
the Supreme Court's inherent and exclusive
power to regulate conduct of attorneys and
violates the State Constitution?

Government Code section 87100 et seq. prohibits public
officials in using his official position to influence a
government decision "in which he knows or has reason to know he
has a financial interest." (Gov. Code, section 87100.)
Pursuant to section 87300 of the Government Code, the
California Department of Justice, and other agencies are
required to adopt and promulgate a Conflict of Interest Code.

The Department of Justice has adopted such a code and
it is implemented by the "1980-81 Manual for Statements of
Economic Interests for Designated Employees form 730 (JUS 501
(1/81))."

By this Act, I was determined to be a "designated"
attorney and ordered to file an "annual economic disclosure
statement" by April 1, 1982 under a penalty of $10.00 per day.
(Exh. B, Gov. Code section 8103(a).) I have made the necessary
filings in a timely manner, but it was made under seal pending
your determination of the question posed herein.

Recent cases have called into question the validity of
a legislative act that infringes upon the state Supreme Court's
inherent and exclusive power to regulate conduct of attorneys.
(See Ballou v. State Ethics Commission (1981) 424 A.2d 983;
Kremer v. State Ethics Commission (1981) 424 A.2d 963.) In
each instance, the court found that the state Supreme Court had
inherent and exclusive power to govern admission, conduct and
discipline of attorneys and that the financial disclosure
requirement of the State Ethics Act, as applied to attorneys
unconstitutionally infringes upon that power.

In California, the judicial power is vested in the
judiciary (Cal. Const. Art. VI, section 1) which has the
inherent and exclusive power to govern admission, conduct and
discipline or attorneys through the State Bar of California.
(Cal. Const. Art. VI, section 9.) Pursuant to its authority,
an elaborate Rules of Professional Conduct has been adopted by
the Board of Governors of the State Bar of California under the
State Bar Act. These rules have been approved by the Supreme
Court of California and are binding upon all members of the
state bar (every person admitted and licensed to practice law
in California must be a member of the state bar). The wilful
breach of the rules are punishable as provided by law.

Included within these rules are provisions governing
an attorney's conduct with regard to conflict of interest.
(See Rules 2-108 (financial arrangements among lawyers), 4-101
(accepting employment adverse to a client), 5-101 (avoiding
adverse interest), 5-102 (avoiding the representation of
adverse interest), etc.) Government attorneys are specifically
singled out by these provisions with respect to their
professional conduct. (See Rule 7-102.)

It is quite apparent that the financial disclosure
requirements of the California Government Conflict of Interests
Act is also constrained by the exclusivity of the Supreme Court
of California to govern the conduct of members of its Bar. In
this regard, the principles enunciated in Ballou and Kremer
have equal application herein. Therefore it is submitted that
California's financial disclosure requirement of the Government
Conflict of Interests Act as implemented by the Department of
Justice is unconstitutional insofar as it infringes upon the
Supreme Court's exclusive power, under the state constitution,
to regulate the conduct of members of the Bar.

Your immediate attention to this matter would be
greatly appreciated.

Very truly yours,

[Signature]
OWEN LEE KWONG
Deputy Attorney General
(213) 736-2106

OLK:lks

cc: Robert Blasier, Jr., Enforcement
Fair Political Practices Commission
Southern Regional Office
James D. Mosman, Code Filing Officer
Scott W. Thorpe, Deputy Attorney General-Election Law

posed: 
Whether the financial disclosure 
requirements of the Government Conflict of 
Interests Act (Gov. Code, section 87100 et 
~.) as implemented in the California 
Department of Justice "1980-81 Manual for 
Statements of Economic Interests for 
Designated Employees form 730 (JUS 501 
(1/81» ," as applied to Department of 
Justice designated attorneys, infringes upon

Government Code section 87100 et seq. prohibits public 
officials in using his official position to influence a 
government decision "in which he knows or has reason to know he 
has a financial interest." 
(Gov. Code, section 87100.) 
Pursuant to section 87300 of the Government Code, the

required to adopt and promulgate a Conflict of Interest Code. 
The Department of Justice has adopted such a code and 
it is implemented by the "1980-81 Manual for Statements of 
Economic Interests for Designated Employees form 730 (JUS 501 
(1/81»." 
(Exh.A.) 
By this Act, I was determined to be a "designated" 
attorney and ordered to file an "annual economic disclosure 
statement" by April 1, 1982 under a penalty of $10.00 per day. 
(Exh. B, Gov. Code section 9l03(a).) 
I have made the necessary 
filings in a timely manner, but it was made under seal pending 
your determination of the question posed herein. 


Recent cases have called into question the validity of 
a legislative act that infringes upon the state Supreme Court's 
inherent and exclusive power to regulate conduct of attorneys. 
(See Ballou v. State Ethics Commission (1981) 424 A.2d 983; 
Kremer v. State Ethics Commission (1981) 424 A.2d 963.) 
In 
each instance, the court found that the state Supreme Court had 
inherent and exclusive power to govern admission, conduct and 
discipline of attorneys and that the financial disclosure 
requirement of the State Ethics Act, as applied to attorneys 
unconstitutionally infringes upon that power. 
In California, the judicial power is vested in the 
judiciary (Cal. Const. Art. VI, section 1) which has the 
inherent and exclusive power to govern admission, conduct and 
discipline or attorneys through the State Bar of California. 
(Cal. Const. Art. VI, section 9.) 
Pursuant to its authority, 
an elaborate Rules of Professional Conduct has been adopted by 
the Board of Governors of the State Bar of California under the 
State Bar Act. 
These rules have been approved by the Supreme 
Court of California and are binding upon all members of the 
state bar (every person admitted and licensed to practice law 
in California must be a member of the state bar). 
The wilful 
breach of the rules are punishable as provided by law. 
Included within these rules are provisions governing 
an attorney's conduct with regard to conflict of interest. 
(See Rules 2-108 (financial arrangements among lawyers), 4-101 
(accepting employment adverse to a client), 5-101 (avoiding 
adverse interest), 5-102 (avoiding the representation of 
adverse interest), etc.) 
Government attorneys are specifically 
singled out by these provisions with respect to their 
professional conduct. 
(See Rule 7-102.) 
It is quite apparent that the financial disclosure 
requirements of the California Government Conflict of Interests 
Act is also constrained by the exclusivity of the Supreme Court 
of California to govern the conduct of members of its Bar. 
In 
this regard, the principles enunciated in Ballou and Kremer 
have equal application herein. 
Therefore it is submitted that 
California's financial disclosure requirement of the Government 
Conflict of Interests Act as implemented by the Department of 
Justice is unconstitutional insofar as it infringes upon the 
Supreme Court's exclusive power, under the state constitution, 
to regulate the conduct of members of the Bar. 


Your immediate attention to this matter would be 
greatly appreciated.

sections.conclusion: The Commission's answer to Mr. Kwong's question must be that he is required to make financial disclosure under the Act. The Political Reform Act explicitly provides that government attorneys (district attorneys as well as the attorney general) are covered by its disclosure and disqualification provisions (Government Code Sections 82003, 82004, 82019, 82024, 87200, 87302). To exclude government attorneys from coverage would contradict long-standing Commission policy and practice.
sections.question_synthetic: Does the requirement that government attorneys file financial disclosure statements under the Political Reform Act violate the California State Constitution by infringing on the California Supreme Court's exclusive authority to regulate attorney conduct?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that Mr. Kwong is required to file financial disclosures under the Political Reform Act because the statute explicitly covers government attorneys, and excluding them would violate established Commission policy and raise independent constitutional problems under Article 3, Section 3.5 of the State Constitution.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document contains both the incoming opinion request from Owen Lee Kwong (dated March 30, 1982) and the FPPC staff memorandum responding to it (dated April 13, 1982). The CONCLUSION field represents the FPPC staff recommendation rather than a formal conclusion section, but captures the substantive answer being recommended. OCR quality is poor but key sections are readable.

CONCLUSION: The Commission's answer to Mr. Kwong's question must be that he is required to make financial disclosure under the Act. The Political Reform Act explicitly provides that government attorneys (district attorneys as well as the attorney general) are covered by its disclosure and disqualification provisions (Government Code Sections 82003, 82004, 82019, 82024, 87200, 87302). To exclude government attorneys from coverage would contradict long-standing Commission policy and practice.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to Deputy Attorney General Owen Lee Kwong's opinion request challenging the constitutionality of requiring financial disclosure statements. The FPPC affirms that government attorneys are covered by the Political Reform Act's disclosure requirements and recommends responding with a detailed advice letter explaining the constitutional validity of the challenged provisions.
